justification: All pass criteria met: JWT for mobile and API key for partners with separate ingress points (auth per consumer type). Internal traffic separation fully designed with dedicated Internal Gateway and service mesh. Business logic explicitly excluded from gateway in pitfalls_to_avoid and component boundaries. SPOF risk identified as critical with multi-AZ mitigation. Webhook delivery addressed with dedicated Webhook Dispatcher service including retry, dead letter queue, and HMAC signatures. flexibility_points and rigidity_points both present and well-articulated. Technology recommendation includes Kong, Envoy, and AWS API Gateway with phased implementation guidance covering build vs managed trade-offs.

observations.text: Excellent three-ingress BFF-style topology with clear separation of mobile, partner, and internal traffic paths
Thorough webhook delivery design with exponential backoff, dead letter queue, HMAC signatures, and CloudEvents spec
Header stripping security concern identified and called out as non-negotiable with integration test requirement
Strangler fig migration strategy de-risks the rollout with concrete phased plan
Redis fail-open vs fail-closed trade-off explicitly analyzed with rationale for logistics platform availability
Rigidity points are honest and actionable — identity header contract lock-in and Redis key schema migration cost
Build vs buy trade-off could be more explicit — mentions Kong/Envoy/AWS API Gateway but doesn't deeply compare managed vs self-hosted cost and operational implications

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: Authentication per consumer type (JWT for mobile, API key for partners) designed correctly with separate auth pipelines. Internal traffic separation fully addressed with a dedicated Internal Service Mesh Gateway on a private network. Business logic explicitly excluded from gateway multiple times (component boundaries, pitfalls_to_avoid). SPOF risk identified as critical with detailed mitigation. Webhook delivery for partners addressed via async Webhook Dispatcher with retry logic, HMAC signing, and dead letter queue. flexibility_points and rigidity_points both present and well-articulated. Technology recommendation includes build/buy consideration — explicitly recommends using existing products (Kong, AWS API Gateway, Envoy) over building custom, with rationale.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough component design with clear boundaries — each component has explicit responsibility and boundary statements
Header spoofing risk identified and mitigated with mandatory header stripping — a sophisticated security concern often missed
Strangler Fig migration pattern with X-Auth-Handled header is a practical, low-risk migration strategy
Rate limiter fail-open vs fail-closed trade-off explicitly addressed with a clear recommendation
Webhook delivery is correctly decoupled via event bus rather than placed in the gateway request path
Rigidity points are specific and actionable — header contract lock-in, network isolation invariant, rate limit key migration risk
Phased implementation guidance with 5 clear phases reduces delivery risk
Latency overhead per hop not explicitly quantified in trade-offs (the rubric expected 1-5ms mention), though it is implicitly addressed by recommending battle-tested products

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: JWT for mobile and API key for partners designed as separate auth pipelines; internal traffic explicitly separated via internal gateway/service mesh on private subnet; business logic explicitly excluded from gateway (repeated in pitfalls_to_avoid); SPOF risk identified as critical with multi-AZ mitigation; webhook delivery for partners fully addressed with dedicated Webhook Dispatcher, exponential backoff, and DLQ; flexibility_points and rigidity_points both present and well-reasoned; technology guidance includes multiple options (Kong, Envoy, AWS API Gateway) with build/buy consideration and implementation-agnostic architecture.

observations.text: Two-gateway topology (external + internal/mesh) cleanly separates external and internal traffic at the network level
Webhook delivery fully designed with its own service, retry logic, HMAC signing, dead letter queue, and partner-specific payload transformation
Explicit fail-open vs fail-closed trade-off analysis for rate limiting shows mature operational thinking
Phased migration plan using Strangler Fig pattern reduces rollout risk
Business logic exclusion from gateway stated both in component boundaries and in pitfalls_to_avoid
Rate limit tiers externalized to Partner Registry rather than hardcoded, supporting flexibility without gateway redeployment
Gateway misconfiguration exposing internal services identified as critical risk with network-level enforcement

trials.agent_output_excerpt: {"executive_summary":"The logistics platform needs a unified API gateway layer to centralize authentication, rate limiting, and traffic isolation that are currently scattered inconsistently across individual services. The design introduces a two-gateway topology: an external gateway handling mobile and partner traffic with differentiated auth and rate limiting, and an internal gateway (or service mesh sidecar) for inter-service communication.
